sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module Thirteen — Research Skills and MLA Basics. Today you learn to tell credible sources from junk, to weave them into your own writing with paraphrase and quotation, and to format an MLA Works Cited page that a teacher will accept without a single red mark.

Here is the trap. You search a topic, you find a blog post that sounds confident, you paste two sentences into your essay, and you write — according to the internet. That sentence loses points in every English class in America. Research is not finding agreement online. Research is choosing sources you can defend, and showing your reader exactly where every idea came from.

Game plan. First, the CRAAP test — five questions that decide whether a source is worth using. Second, the difference between paraphrasing and quoting, and when to use each. Third, MLA in-text citations and the Works Cited page, with the exact format. Two pauses on real assignment-style work.

CRAAP is the standard high-school credibility test. Five letters. Currency — is the information recent enough for your topic? Relevance — does it actually answer your question? Authority — who wrote it, and what are their qualifications? Accuracy — can the claims be verified in another source? Purpose — is the page informing you, or selling you something? A source needs to pass all five. One fail is enough to walk away.

Two ways to bring a source into your writing. Paraphrase — restate the author's idea fully in your own words and your own sentence structure, then cite. Direct quote — copy the exact wording inside quotation marks, then cite. Paraphrase by default. Quote only when the author's exact phrasing matters — a definition, a striking phrase, or a number you cannot rewrite without distortion.

MLA in-text citation is short. Inside parentheses, author last name and page number, no comma between. Smith forty-two. If you already name the author in your sentence, just the page number in parentheses. The citation goes before the period, not after. Every paraphrase and every direct quote needs one. No exceptions.

The Works Cited page lives on its own page at the end. Title — Works Cited — centered, not bold, not underlined. Entries in alphabetical order by the author's last name. Every entry uses a hanging indent — first line flush left, every line after indented half an inch. Double-spaced throughout, with no extra space between entries. Same font as the rest of the essay.

Your turn. You see three sources on the slide — a 2024 university library article, an anonymous personal blog from 2009, and a 2023 nonprofit research report. Pause now. For each one, run the CRAAP test and decide — use, or do not use. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Source one — university library article, 2024, written by a named professor — use it. Currency, relevance, authority, accuracy, and purpose all check out. Source two — anonymous blog, 2009, no author credentials, no sources cited — do not use. Authority and accuracy both fail before you even check currency. Source three — nonprofit research report, 2023, with named authors and a methodology section — use it. Pass on all five.

Three Works Cited entries cover most of what English One asks for. Book — last name, first name, title in italics, publisher, year. Website article — last name, first name, quotation marks around the article title, italicized website name, publication date, the URL or DOI. Magazine or journal article — last name, first name, quotation marks around the article title, italicized journal name, volume and issue, year, page range. Memorize the order — author, title, container, year, location.

One more. The slide shows three raw sources you have just gathered for your research paragraph. Pause now. Write the three Works Cited entries in correct MLA format. Then arrange them in the order they would appear on your Works Cited page. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Alphabetical order means Adler, then Chen, then the Pew Research Center. Adler comma Mortimer J., How to Read a Book, Touchstone, 2014. Chen comma Lin, quote The Quiet Reader, end quote, italicized The Atlantic, March 2, 2023, then the URL. Pew Research Center, quote Teens, Social Media, and Reading, end quote, italicized Pew Research Center, 2022, then the URL. Three entries. Hanging indent on every one. Double-spaced. That is a Works Cited page that earns full credit.

Here is your assignment. Choose a research topic of your own — anything you genuinely want to know more about. Collect three sources — one book or ebook, one website, and one article from a periodical or database. Run CRAAP on each. Then write a three-hundred-word informational paragraph that paraphrases at least two of the sources and directly quotes at least one, every borrowing with an MLA in-text citation. Attach a properly formatted MLA Works Cited page with all three entries.

Recap. CRAAP — five questions, one fail and you walk away. Paraphrase by default, quote only when the exact words matter, and cite both. In-text citations are author last name and page number in parentheses, before the period. Works Cited — centered title, alphabetical order, hanging indent, double-spaced. Every borrowed idea needs a citation. No exceptions.

How to actually master this. Read the Purdue Online Writing Lab pages on MLA general format and on the Works Cited examples for books, articles, and websites. Practice the CRAAP test on three sources you find yourself — keep the worksheet your advisor gave you. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the three-hundred-word research paragraph with the Works Cited page attached. Book an advisor check-in if any source feels borderline. Next up — your English One end-of-course portfolio review.
